CXT.thesis_snapshot: BULL: CXT's US Currency franchise is a durable statutory monopoly operating through a multi-year high-denomination print cycle — the 2026 Federal Reserve order covers 3.8–5.1 billion notes with high-denomination notes up ~90% YoY and the new $10 bill launching in Q4 2026; the De La Rue Authentication (£300M, May 2025) and Antares Vision (€362M, April 2026) acquisitions position CXT as a global authentication platform, and the 2028 targets of ~$2.5B revenue with mid-20s% adj EBITDA imply a stock worth ~$90+. BEAR: The acquisitions are thesis-changing bets not yet proven — leverage rose to 2.9x with integration risk across multiple technology stacks and geographies; CPI's vending/kiosk end-market faces structural headwinds from cashless payment migration; Q1 2026 GAAP margins collapsed to 5.7% from 11.3% on acquisition amortization loads; and the 2026 adj EPS guidance midpoint of $4.25 was 5.5% below consensus expectations, triggering the de-rating. PRICED IN: At ~11x FY2026 adj EPS ($4.10–$4.40 guidance), the stock prices in neither the US currency cycle upswing nor any accretion from the two large acquisitions — 5 of 6 analysts have Buy ratings with average targets of ~$66–67 (street high: $85). The 29.5% drawdown from $69 reflects guidance-cut risk and acquisition integration uncertainty rather than structural deterioration in the core banknote and currency businesses.

HDSN.thesis_snapshot: Hudson is the dominant US refrigerant reclaimer entering a multi-decade structural tailwind: the AIM Act mandates 85% HFC virgin production phasedown by 2036 with major step-downs in 2029, creating irreversible scarcity for virgin supply and elevating reclaimed refrigerant as the primary legal source for servicing existing HVAC equipment. Yet the stock has fallen 47% from its September 2025 high to $5.56, hitting 0.98x book value. Six insiders bought near the 52-week low in May 2026 alongside a $2.5M corporate buyback. The trough story: FY2025 gross margin fell to 25.2% (from 27.7%), Q4 2025 delivered a $4.2M inventory write-down (crashing Q4 margin to 8%), and Q1 2026 missed estimates by 84% on trough HFC pricing. All are pricing/inventory cycle issues, not regulatory reversals. The bull case is the 2029 step-down tightening supply; the bear case is that HFC prices stay depressed for 3+ years before that catalyst fires.

INFU.thesis_snapshot: Bull: InfuSystem is a capital-light infrastructure layer between oncologists and home infusion, with nearly 840 contracted payer networks, a proprietary pump fleet, same-day delivery logistics at seven U.S./Canada locations, and meaningful EBITDA margin expansion (14.4% in 2024 to 17.6% in 2025) driven by mix-shift toward higher-margin Patient Services; the GE Healthcare contract restructure ($7.1M annual headwind) is already absorbed in guidance, and CEO Lachance alongside two other insiders purchased $131K in May 2026 at sub-$200M market cap. Bear: Q1 2026 revenue declined 3.0% YoY due to the GE contract reset plus lapping a one-time software-update contract, Device Solutions is down 17% YoY, CMS competitive bidding and fee-schedule risk is perennial for a DME supplier, and at 8.6x EV/EBITDA much of the near-term margin story is reflected. The bear case is partially priced in — the 9% drawdown from the 52-week high and a sub-9x EV/EBITDA multiple for a growing healthcare services business suggest modest under-valuation if Patient Services growth is sustained and Q1 2026 Device headwinds are transitory.

J.thesis_snapshot: BULL: Jacobs is executing a deliberate pivot from legacy construction services to high-margin consulting and critical technology infrastructure — AI/data center project backlog doubled year-over-year, the $27B total backlog (up 22%) reflects strong Infrastructure Investment Act and compute-buildout demand, and the FY2026 adj EPS guidance of $7.10–$7.35 (+18%) implies meaningful margin expansion that the 25% drawdown from the 52-week high has not credited. BEAR: PA Consulting integration risk is real — combining a UK advisory firm with a US engineering conglomerate is culturally and operationally complex; hyper-scale data center and semiconductor fab projects carry construction-phase risk; and loss of federal civilian agency work under spending reviews could weigh on the 30% government-facing revenue. PRICED IN: At ~17x forward adj EPS the stock trades at a discount to comparable high-value engineering/consulting peers (20x+), suggesting the market has penalized Jacobs for integration overhang rather than acknowledging the fundamental revenue quality improvement underway.

MRP.thesis_snapshot: At $30.93, MRP trades at ~11x FY2026 earnings with a 9.5–10% dividend yield backed by contractually fixed fee income — not NAV-dependent. The bull case is structural: the US housing supply deficit is 4.7M units and homebuilders are permanently shifting to asset-light land models that require MRP's capital. Q1 2026 revenue surged to $194.9M from $82.7M a year earlier, non-Lennar counterparties grew to 17, and $0.77/share quarterly dividend was declared (>$3/yr). The bear case is real: Lennar concentration means one client's pullback directly hits cash flows, floating-rate borrowings at SOFR+2% on a $1.835B credit facility compress margins if rates stay high, and a severe housing downturn could slow takedowns faster than option agreements allow for adjustment. The decisive call: at 11x with a 9.5% yield and a 5 Buy/0 Sell sell-side consensus at $38.60 avg target, the bear case is well-known and more than priced in.

PSN.thesis_snapshot: BULL: The record $6.6B funded backlog and $11B total backlog — built over years of strategic positioning in cyber resilience, missile defense, and space — provide multi-year earnings visibility even after the contract setbacks; Parsons is well-positioned for the DoD modernization budget cycle and growing cyber/electronic warfare demand, and insider buying confirms board conviction at the trough. BEAR: Three sequential shocks — a ~$1B confidential IC contract rolloff disclosed only retrospectively, loss of the FAA Air Traffic Control modernization contract to Peraton on December 5, 2025, and a Q4 FY2025 revenue miss — raise fundamental questions about whether contract replacement can keep pace with losses, especially under DOGE-era federal spending scrutiny. PRICED IN: The 43% drawdown from $89.50 has re-rated PSN from 15x to ~8x forward EBITDA, fully penalizing the lost contracts but leaving no credit for the robust remaining backlog and the growing electronic warfare / space pipeline; at current levels the bear case requires no recovery in contract wins, which seems unlikely given Parsons' security-cleared workforce moat.

VITL.thesis_snapshot: Ten insiders including CEO William Cyr bought $417k of VITL in May 2026 right after the stock's worst quarterly report in years — Q1 2026 delivered the first quarterly net loss (-$1.5M vs +$16.9M prior year) as avian flu inflated egg costs and gross margin collapsed from 38.5% to 28.3%. The stock at $11.19 is 79% below its August 2025 high of $53.125 and trades at just 0.61x TTM sales and 10x depressed earnings — priced for margin impairment to be permanent. The bull case: pasture-raised egg supply normalization will restore structural 38–40% gross margins, the $82M capex cycle in FY2025 is peak (new processing plant reduces input costs), and revenue is still growing 15% even in the worst egg cost environment in decades. The bear case: avian flu proves more persistent than expected, capex-driven FCF of -$90M TTM may require a dilutive equity raise, and the butter exit adds write-down charges through FY2026.

VTSI.thesis_snapshot: Bull: At a $37M market cap with $17.9M in cash, the enterprise value is only ~$18M — less than 1x EV/Sales if revenue normalizes toward the FY2023 peak of $38.8M — and a $25.2M backlog (nearly 70% of annual revenue) is expected to convert mostly in H2 2026 as federal JAG/COPS grant programs reopen; the STEP subscription program (95% renewal, 19% of FY2025 revenue) provides a growing recurring floor. Bear: Revenue has declined three consecutive years ($38.8M → $26.4M → $22.4M → annualizing ~$15M from the Q1 2026 run-rate), the 79%+ government concentration makes the business structurally dependent on discretionary federal spending that has been actively cut under the current administration, and if the H2 2026 recovery fails to materialize cash could erode toward the share price. The bear case is partially but not fully priced in: the cash cushion provides multi-year runway, but if federal funding remains constrained through 2027, book value itself (~$3.92/share) could compress toward the current share price, offering limited margin of safety beyond cash.
